topic_text: Identifying Linear Relationships in Scatterplots (Positive, Negative, None)
skills_targeted: classifying the direction of a linear association from a described real-world trend
recognizing that 'as x increases, y increases' defines a positive association
question_text: A researcher records the number of hours students study and their test scores. As study hours increase, test scores tend to increase as well. Which best describes the linear association between study hours and test score?

answer_choices.D: The direction cannot be determined without calculating the correlation coefficient.

explanation: A positive linear association means that as one variable increases, the other tends to increase. Here, more study hours go with higher test scores, so as the x-variable rises the y-variable rises with it. That is the definition of a positive association.

distractor_logic.A: Correct: as study hours increase, test scores tend to increase, which is a positive linear association.
distractor_logic.B: Student reverses the meaning of the trend, labeling an 'increase together' pattern as negative.
distractor_logic.C: Student assumes that any imperfect, real-world scatter means no association exists at all.
distractor_logic.D: Student believes a direction cannot be named without first computing a numeric correlation coefficient, when the described trend is already sufficient.

strategy_hints.hint_text: Focus on the direction words: do the two quantities move the same way or opposite ways? Match that movement to positive, negative, or none.

author: jd-oviedo

topic_text: Identifying Linear Relationships in Scatterplots (Positive, Negative, None)
skills_targeted: classifying the direction of a linear association from a described inverse real-world trend
recognizing that 'as x increases, y decreases' defines a negative association
question_text: A utility company finds that as the average outdoor temperature increases, the amount households spend on home heating decreases. Which best describes the linear association between outdoor temperature and heating cost?

answer_choices.D: The direction cannot be determined without seeing the scatterplot.

explanation: A negative linear association means that as one variable increases, the other tends to decrease. Here, higher temperatures go with lower heating costs, so as x rises, y falls. That is a negative association.

distractor_logic.A: Student sees two variables changing and defaults to 'positive' without checking that they move in opposite directions.
distractor_logic.B: Correct: as temperature increases, heating cost decreases, which is a negative linear association.
distractor_logic.C: Student assumes imperfect real-world data means no association exists.
distractor_logic.D: Student believes the verbal description is not enough and a plotted image is required to name the direction.

strategy_hints.hint_text: One quantity goes up while the other goes down. Decide whether 'up while the other goes down' matches positive, negative, or none.

author: jd-oviedo

topic_text: Identifying Linear Relationships in Scatterplots (Positive, Negative, None)
skills_targeted: recognizing the absence of a linear trend from a described scatter
distinguishing 'no linear association' from a positive or negative direction
question_text: A study compares students' shoe sizes to their scores on a history exam. When the data are plotted, the points are scattered with no overall upward or downward trend. Which best describes the linear association between shoe size and exam score?

answer_choices.D: The association is positive only because both variables are measured with positive numbers.

explanation: An association exists only when the points show a consistent upward or downward trend. Here the points are scattered with no overall direction, so there is no linear association between shoe size and exam score.

distractor_logic.A: Student over-reads a few high-high points as an upward trend instead of judging the overall pattern.
distractor_logic.B: Student over-reads a few points as a downward trend instead of judging the overall pattern.
distractor_logic.C: Correct: the points show no consistent upward or downward trend, so there is no linear association.
distractor_logic.D: Student confuses the sign of the data values (all positive numbers) with the direction of the association.

strategy_hints.hint_text: Ask whether the points as a whole drift up, drift down, or drift in no clear direction at all.
If there is no overall up-or-down pattern, you can rule out any choice that commits to a direction.

author: jd-oviedo

topic_text: Identifying Linear Relationships in Scatterplots (Positive, Negative, None)
skills_targeted: determining the overall trend direction from an explicit set of data points
recognizing that a linear association does not require equal increments
question_text: A data set is given as the points (1, 4), (2, 6), (3, 9), (4, 11), and (5, 14). Which best describes the linear association between x and y?

answer_choices.A: There is no linear association because the y-values do not increase by the same amount each time.
answer_choices.C: The direction cannot be determined from only five points.

explanation: As x increases from 1 to 5, the y-values increase throughout: 4, 6, 9, 11, 14. Because y consistently rises as x rises, the association is positive. A linear association does not require the increase to be exactly the same size each step.

distractor_logic.A: Student believes a linear association requires a constant difference between consecutive y-values, so the unequal gaps (2, 3, 2, 3) read as 'no association.'
distractor_logic.B: Student reverses the direction of the clearly increasing values.
distractor_logic.C: Student believes five points are too few to identify a trend.
distractor_logic.D: Correct: y increases throughout as x increases (4, 6, 9, 11, 14), which is a positive association.

strategy_hints.hint_text: List the y-values in order as x goes 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Are they generally climbing, generally falling, or neither?

author: jd-oviedo

topic_text: Identifying Linear Relationships in Scatterplots (Positive, Negative, None)
skills_targeted: determining the overall trend direction from an explicit set of data points
recognizing a negative association from consistently decreasing y-values
question_text: A data set is given as the points (1, 20), (2, 17), (3, 15), (4, 12), and (5, 9). Which best describes the linear association between x and y?

answer_choices.C: There is no linear association because the y-values do not decrease by the same amount each time.
answer_choices.D: The direction cannot be determined without the correlation coefficient.

explanation: As x increases from 1 to 5, the y-values decrease throughout: 20, 17, 15, 12, 9. Because y consistently falls as x rises, the association is negative. The decrease does not need to be the same size each step for the association to be linear in direction.

distractor_logic.A: Correct: y decreases throughout as x increases (20, 17, 15, 12, 9), which is a negative association.
distractor_logic.B: Student reverses the direction of the clearly decreasing values.
distractor_logic.C: Student believes equal decrements are required for a linear association, so the unequal gaps (3, 2, 3, 3) read as 'none.'
distractor_logic.D: Student believes a numeric correlation coefficient is required to name the direction.

strategy_hints.hint_text: Read the y-values in order as x climbs. If they trend downward overall, the direction is negative.

author: jd-oviedo

topic_text: Identifying Linear Relationships in Scatterplots (Positive, Negative, None)
skills_targeted: judging the strength of a linear association from how tightly points cluster
distinguishing a strong association from a weak one in the same direction
question_text: A scatterplot has an x-axis labeled 'Hours Practiced' (ranging 0 to 12) and a y-axis labeled 'Free Throws Made out of 50' (ranging 0 to 50). The points lie very close to a single straight line rising from about (1, 8) to about (11, 46), with very little scatter and no outliers. Which best describes the linear association?
explanation: Direction is positive because the points rise from left to right. Strength is judged by how tightly the points cluster around a line: here they hug the line closely with little scatter, so the association is strong. Together that is a strong positive association.

distractor_logic.A: Student underestimates strength, treating any plot as 'weak' even when the points cluster tightly along the line.
distractor_logic.B: Correct: the points hug an upward line with little scatter, indicating a strong positive association.
distractor_logic.C: Student reverses the direction of an upward-rising pattern.
distractor_logic.D: Student believes points must fall exactly on the line for any association to exist.

strategy_hints.hint_text: Decide direction first (do points rise or fall?), then judge strength by how closely the points hug a line.

author: jd-oviedo

topic_text: Identifying Linear Relationships in Scatterplots (Positive, Negative, None)
skills_targeted: distinguishing a weak association from no association when scatter is heavy
recognizing that a real direction can exist even with wide scatter
question_text: A scatterplot has an x-axis labeled 'Daily Steps (thousands)' (ranging 0 to 15) and a y-axis labeled 'Hours of Sleep' (ranging 4 to 10). The points drift gently upward from left to right but are widely scattered, with no tight line. Which best describes the linear association?
explanation: The points trend upward, so the direction is positive. But they are widely scattered rather than hugging a line, so the strength is weak. Together that is a weak positive association. The wide scatter does not erase the real upward direction.

distractor_logic.A: Student overstates strength, reading a faint upward drift as a tight relationship.
distractor_logic.B: Student misreads the direction of an upward drift.
distractor_logic.C: Correct: the points trend gently upward but with wide scatter, indicating a weak positive association.
distractor_logic.D: Student confuses 'weak' with 'none,' ignoring the real upward direction present despite the heavy scatter.

strategy_hints.hint_text: Separate two questions: which way do the points tilt, and how tightly do they hug a line?
A faint but real tilt rules out 'no association,' and heavy scatter rules out 'strong.'

author: jd-oviedo

topic_text: Identifying Linear Relationships in Scatterplots (Positive, Negative, None)
skills_targeted: recognizing a curved (non-linear) pattern as having no single linear direction
resisting the urge to force a positive or negative label onto a U-shaped pattern
question_text: A scatterplot has an x-axis labeled 'Average Daily Temperature (°F)' (ranging 30 to 100) and a y-axis labeled 'Monthly Energy Bill ($)' (ranging 0 to 250). The points form a clear U-shape: bills are high (around \$200) at low temperatures, drop to a low (around \$80) near 65–70°F, then rise again to high (around \$200) at high temperatures. Which best describes the linear association?

answer_choices.C: The association is linear but switches from negative to positive at the midpoint.
answer_choices.D: There is no linear association, though a clear curved (non-linear) pattern exists.

explanation: The points clearly follow a U-shaped pattern, so a relationship exists, but it is not linear. A line can only point one consistent direction, and these points fall then rise. The correct description is that there is no linear association even though there is an obvious non-linear pattern.

distractor_logic.A: Student focuses only on the rising right half of the U and calls the whole pattern positive.
distractor_logic.B: Student focuses only on the falling left half of the U and calls the whole pattern negative.
distractor_logic.C: Student tries to force a single linear label onto a curved pattern by claiming a linear relationship can change direction.
distractor_logic.D: Correct: the points follow a clear U-shaped non-linear pattern, so there is no single linear association.

strategy_hints.hint_text: Ask whether a single straight line could reasonably pass through this shape, or whether the points bend.
Sketch the described shape. If it curves down then back up, no single straight line fits it.

author: jd-oviedo

topic_text: Identifying Linear Relationships in Scatterplots (Positive, Negative, None)
skills_targeted: judging strength by clustering rather than by the steepness of the trend
classifying a tight downward pattern as strong negative
question_text: A scatterplot has an x-axis labeled 'Outdoor Temperature (°F)' (ranging 30 to 90) and a y-axis labeled 'Cups of Hot Chocolate Sold' (ranging 0 to 60). The points cluster tightly along a line that falls from left to right, with little scatter. Which best describes the linear association?
explanation: The points fall from left to right, so the direction is negative. They cluster tightly along the line, so the strength is strong. Strength is about how closely points hug the line, not how steep the line looks. Together this is a strong negative association.

distractor_logic.A: Correct: points hug a downward line tightly, indicating a strong negative association.
distractor_logic.B: Student confuses the visual gentleness of the slope with weak strength; strength comes from tight clustering, not steepness.
distractor_logic.C: Student reverses the direction of a downward pattern.
distractor_logic.D: Student believes any scatter at all rules out an association.

strategy_hints.hint_text: Steepness is not strength. Judge strength only by how closely the points hug the line, then add the direction.

author: jd-oviedo

topic_text: Identifying Linear Relationships in Scatterplots (Positive, Negative, None)
skills_targeted: recognizing a flat, trendless horizontal band as no linear association
resisting over-reading random noise as a weak trend
question_text: A scatterplot has an x-axis labeled 'Student ID Number' (ranging 1000 to 1100) and a y-axis labeled 'Quiz Score' (ranging 0 to 100). The points are scattered in a roughly horizontal band centered near 75, showing no overall upward or downward tilt. Which best describes the linear association?
explanation: The points form a flat horizontal band with no tilt up or down, so there is no linear association between student ID number and quiz score. A horizontal cloud of points means changes in x do not correspond to any consistent change in y.

distractor_logic.A: Student over-reads random scatter as a slight upward trend.
distractor_logic.B: Correct: the points form a flat, trendless horizontal band, so there is no linear association.
distractor_logic.C: Student over-reads random scatter as a slight downward trend.
distractor_logic.D: Student over-reads a few higher points as evidence of a strong upward trend.

strategy_hints.hint_text: A flat band has no overall tilt. Ask whether y systematically changes as x increases, or stays level.
If there is no overall tilt, every choice that names a direction can be ruled out.

author: jd-oviedo

topic_text: Identifying Linear Relationships in Scatterplots (Positive, Negative, None)
skills_targeted: reasoning about how a contrary outlier affects the strength of a trend
predicting the effect of removing a point that runs against the pattern
question_text: A scatterplot has an x-axis labeled 'Hours of Screen Time' (ranging 0 to 10) and a y-axis labeled 'Hours of Sleep' (ranging 3 to 10). Most points form a clear downward trend (more screen time goes with less sleep), but one point sits in the upper right at about (9, 9), showing high screen time and high sleep, against the trend. How does removing this upper-right point affect the association?

answer_choices.A: Removing it changes the association from negative to positive.
answer_choices.B: Removing it weakens the negative association.
answer_choices.C: Removing it strengthens the negative association.
answer_choices.D: Removing it has no effect on the association.

explanation: The upper-right point runs against the overall downward trend. A point that contradicts the pattern weakens it while present. Removing that contrary point leaves the remaining points hugging the downward line more tightly, so the negative association becomes stronger.

distractor_logic.A: Student overstates a single outlier's influence, assuming removing one point can flip the overall direction.
distractor_logic.B: Student reverses the effect, treating the contrary point as if it were supporting the trend.
distractor_logic.C: Correct: the outlier opposes the downward trend, so removing it makes the remaining negative association tighter and stronger.
distractor_logic.D: Student assumes a single point can never affect an association.

strategy_hints.hint_text: Decide whether the lone point agrees with or fights the overall trend, then ask what happens when a fighting point is removed.
Picture the cloud with and without that one upper-right point. Does the remaining pattern get tighter or looser?

author: jd-oviedo

topic_text: Identifying Linear Relationships in Scatterplots (Positive, Negative, None)
skills_targeted: comparing two associations of the same direction by strength
using clustering rather than spread or count to judge strength
question_text: Two scatterplots use the same axes: x labeled 'Study Hours' (0 to 10) and y labeled 'Test Score' (50 to 100). In Plot 1 the points lie very close to a straight upward line. In Plot 2 the points also trend upward but are widely scattered around the line. Both associations are positive. Which plot shows the stronger linear association?

answer_choices.A: Plot 1, because its points lie closer to a straight line.
answer_choices.B: Plot 2, because its points span a wider range of x-values.
answer_choices.C: The two are equally strong because both associations are positive.
answer_choices.D: Plot 2, because it appears to contain more points.

explanation: Strength is measured by how tightly the points cluster around a line. Plot 1's points hug the line closely, while Plot 2's are widely scattered. Both are positive, but Plot 1 is the stronger linear association.

distractor_logic.A: Correct: strength is judged by how tightly points cluster around a line, and Plot 1's tighter cluster is stronger.
distractor_logic.B: Student confuses the spread of x-values with the strength of the association.
distractor_logic.C: Student confuses direction with strength, assuming the same direction means equal strength.
distractor_logic.D: Student confuses the number of points with the strength of the association.

strategy_hints.hint_text: Strength is about tightness around a line, not spread, count, or direction. Compare how closely each plot's points hug the line.
Rule out any reason that points to spread, number of points, or shared direction rather than tightness.

author: jd-oviedo

topic_text: Identifying Linear Relationships in Scatterplots (Positive, Negative, None)
skills_targeted: distinguishing an approximately linear pattern from a curved one
recognizing a positive direction that is nonetheless non-linear
question_text: A scatterplot has an x-axis labeled 'Years of Experience' (ranging 0 to 30) and a y-axis labeled 'Annual Salary (in \$1000s)' (ranging 30 to 120). The points rise steeply at first and then flatten out as experience increases, forming a curve that bends downward as it climbs. Which best describes the relationship?

answer_choices.B: No association, since the points do not form a straight line.
answer_choices.C: Negative association, because the curve flattens.
answer_choices.D: A positive association that is not linear.

explanation: Salary increases with experience throughout, so the direction is positive. But the rate of increase changes: steep at first, then flattening. Because the points bend rather than follow a straight line, the relationship is positive but not linear.

distractor_logic.A: Student labels any upward trend 'linear,' ignoring the clear curvature shown by the changing rate of increase.
distractor_logic.B: Student assumes that if a relationship is not a straight line, no association exists at all.
distractor_logic.C: Student misreads the flattening (a slowing increase) as an actual decrease.
distractor_logic.D: Correct: y rises with x throughout (positive), but the changing rate of increase means the pattern is curved, not linear.

strategy_hints.hint_text: Separate direction from shape: the points can keep rising (positive) while still bending away from a straight line (non-linear).
Sketch a line that bends and flattens. Does a single straight line fit it well, or does the curve pull away?

author: jd-oviedo

topic_text: Identifying Linear Relationships in Scatterplots (Positive, Negative, None)
skills_targeted: recognizing when an apparent trend is driven by a few extreme points
judging the overall association from the bulk of the data, not the extremes
question_text: A scatterplot has an x-axis labeled 'Number of Pets Owned' (ranging 0 to 8) and a y-axis labeled 'Monthly Grocery Spending ($)' (ranging 200 to 800). Most points form a flat, trendless cloud near \$400, but two extreme points stand apart: one near (0, 220) and one near (8, 760), which together create the visual impression of an upward line. Which best describes the linear association?

answer_choices.A: Strong positive association supported by all the data.
answer_choices.B: Little to no linear association; the apparent upward trend is driven by two extreme points.
answer_choices.D: Moderate positive association evenly supported across the data.

explanation: The bulk of the points form a flat cloud with no trend. Only two extreme points, at opposite corners, create the illusion of an upward line. Because the trend disappears once those two leverage points are set aside, the linear association is minimal.

distractor_logic.A: Student trusts the two extreme points as if the entire data set rose along with them.
distractor_logic.B: Correct: the bulk of the points show no trend; two extreme points create the illusion of a line, so the real association is minimal.
distractor_logic.C: Student misreads the direction of the apparent pattern entirely.
distractor_logic.D: Student assumes the upward impression is supported evenly by all points rather than by two leverage points.

strategy_hints.hint_text: Ask what the middle of the data does on its own. If the trend vanishes without the two corner points, it was never really there.
Any choice claiming the whole data set supports a trend conflicts with a flat central cloud.

author: jd-oviedo

topic_text: Identifying Linear Relationships in Scatterplots (Positive, Negative, None)
skills_targeted: comparing the strength of three associations regardless of direction
recognizing that a negative association can be stronger than a positive one
question_text: Three scatterplots are shown. Plot 1 shows a loose upward cloud (weak positive). Plot 2 shows points clustered tightly along a downward line (strong negative). Plot 3 shows a moderately scattered upward pattern (moderate positive). Which plot shows the strongest linear association?

answer_choices.A: Plot 1, because positive associations are stronger than negative ones.
answer_choices.B: Plot 3, because its points sit nearest the center of the graph.
answer_choices.C: Plot 2, because its points cluster most tightly along a line.
answer_choices.D: Plot 2 is actually the weakest, because a negative slope indicates a weak relationship.

explanation: Strength depends only on how tightly the points cluster along a line, not on direction. Plot 2's points hug the line most tightly, so it is the strongest association even though it is negative.

distractor_logic.A: Student believes positive associations are inherently stronger than negative ones; direction does not determine strength.
distractor_logic.B: Student uses an irrelevant feature (central location) instead of clustering to judge strength.
distractor_logic.C: Correct: strength is about how tightly points cluster along a line, and Plot 2 is tightest, so it is strongest despite being negative.
distractor_logic.D: Student confuses a negative direction with a weak association.

strategy_hints.hint_text: Ignore which way each plot tilts. Compare only how tightly each one's points hug a line.
Rule out any reasoning that ties strength to direction or to where the points sit on the graph.

author: jd-oviedo
